title: John Gilbert (Bushranger)
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ [He and his gang stuck up the township of Canowindra for two days in 1859.]

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  (Air: ן¿½Four and twenty blackbirds baked in a pie.ן¿½)


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ John Gilbert was a bushranger of terrible renown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For sticking lots of people up and shooting others down.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ John Gilbert said unto his pals, ן¿½Although they make a bobbery
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ About our tricks we have never done a tip-top thing in robbery.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We have all of us a fancy for experiments in pillage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet never have we seized a town, or even sacked a village.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ John Gilbert said unto his matesן¿½ן¿½Though partners we have been
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In all rascality, yet we no festal day have seen.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ John Gilbert said he thought he saw no obstacle to hinder a
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Piratical descent upon the town of Canowindra.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So into Canowindra town rode Gilbert and his men,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all the Canowindra folk subsided there and then.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Canowindra populace cried, ן¿½Hereן¿½s a lot of strangers!!!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But immediately recovered when they found they were bushrangers.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Johnny Gilbert said to them, ן¿½You need not be afraid.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are only old companions whom bushrangers you have made.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Johnny Gilbert said, said he, ן¿½Weן¿½ll never hurt a hair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of men who bravely recognise that we are just all there.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The New South Welshmen said at once, not making any fuss,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Johnny Gilbert, after all, was ן¿½Just but one of us.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Johnny Gilbert took the town (including public houses),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And treated all theֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½cockatoosן¿½ and shouted for their spouses.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Miss Oן¿½Flanagan performed in manner quite gintailly
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon the grand planner for the bushranger Oן¿½Meally.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And every stranger passing by they took, and when they got him
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They robbed him of his money and occasionally shot him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Johnnyן¿½s enigmatic feat admits of this solution,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That bushranging in New South Wales is a favoured institution.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So Johnny Gilbert neן¿½er allows an anxious thought to fetch him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For well he knows the Government donן¿½t really want to ketch him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And if such practices should be to New South Welshmen dear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With not the least demurring word ought we to interfere.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
